articles.title: Baboon Sibling Rivalry Suggests Monkeys Feel Jealousy Like People
articles.abstract: Young primates in a southern African nature park were observed to constantly interfere when their mother was giving attention to a younger brother or sister.
org_facet: Proceedings of the Royal Society B (Journal)

articles.title: Lost Soviet Moon Lander May Have Been Found
articles.abstract: Two research teams have identified possible landing sites for Luna 9, the first human-made object to safely reach the lunar surface. “One of them is wrong,” an expert said.

multimedia.caption: A replica of the Luna 9 lander in the Museum of Cosmonautics in Moscow.
A replica of the Luna 9 lander in the Museum of Cosmonautics in Moscow.
A replica of the Luna 9 lander in the Museum of Cosmonautics in Moscow.
A replica of the Luna 9 lander in the Museum of Cosmonautics in Moscow.

articles.title: These Birds Are of Different Feathers, but They Flock Together
articles.abstract: Researchers and volunteers in Texas documented birds of different species preening one another. Experts say the behavior may be more common than documented.

multimedia.caption: An instance of interspecific allopreening, as ornithologists call it, between a crested caracara, right, and a black vulture in San Antonio.
An instance of interspecific allopreening, as ornithologists call it, between a crested caracara, right, and a black vulture in San Antonio.
An instance of interspecific allopreening, as ornithologists call it, between a crested caracara, right, and a black vulture in San Antonio.

articles.title: Why Mountain Lions in California Are Threatened
articles.abstract: Six subpopulations of mountain lions in California face mounting threats, including habitat fragmentation from highways, urban sprawl, and wildfires, as well as widespread rodenticide poisoning. Loren Elliott, a photojournalist for The New York Times, shows how he documents these elusive animals.
articles.byline: By Loren Elliott, Gabriel Blanco and Rebecca Suner

articles.title: War Came to Ukraine and Its Dogs Are Not the Same
articles.abstract: Researchers discovered surprising changes to former pets along the front line of combat with Russia.

multimedia.caption: A dog walked past damaged houses in the city of Svyatohirsk, Donetsk, last summer.
A dog walked past damaged houses in the city of Svyatohirsk, Donetsk, last summer.
A dog walked past damaged houses in the city of Svyatohirsk, Donetsk, last summer.
A dog walked past damaged houses in the city of Svyatohirsk, Donetsk, last summer.

articles.title: These Mathematicians Are Putting A.I. to the Test
articles.abstract: Large language models struggle to solve research-level math questions. It takes a human to assess just how poorly they perform.

multimedia.caption: Martin Hairer, a mathematician at the Swiss Federal Technology Institute of Lausanne. He splits his time between there and the Imperial College London.
multimedia.copyright: Aurelien Bergot for The New York Times

articles.title: Rebuilding the Lighthouse of Alexandria, Block by Virtual Block
articles.abstract: An ancient skyscraper considered the seventh wonder of the world crumbled to ruin centuries ago. Now an ambitious archaeological project aims to reassemble it in 3-D.
org_facet: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization

multimedia.caption: A 19th-century engraved depiction of the Pharos of Alexandria.

articles.title: At a ‘Tea Party’ With Scientists, This Ape Showed Some Imagination
articles.abstract: In a playtime experiment, researchers found that our closest living relatives have the capacity for make-believe, too.

articles.title: Rare Giant Phantom Jelly Spotted in Deep Waters Near Argentina
articles.abstract: Scientists had a rare encounter with a giant phantom jelly during a dive off of Argentina in the Atlantic Ocean.

related_urls.suggested_link_text: This Giant Phantom Jelly Won’t Eat You. Maybe.

articles.title: This Giant Phantom Jelly Won’t Eat You. Maybe.
articles.abstract: During a dive off Argentina, scientists documented a rare jellyfish discovered a little over a century ago and seldom seen since, as well as the fish that keep it company.

articles.title: He Built a Server to Protect Indigenous Health Data
articles.abstract: Joseph Yracheta was in charge of a repository that compiled and protected tribal health data. Then its funding was cut.

multimedia.copyright: Tara Weston for The New York Times
Tara Weston for The New York Times
Tara Weston for The New York Times
Tara Weston for The New York Times

articles.title: NASA Eyes March Artemis II Launch After Hydrogen Leaks Delay Moon Mission
articles.abstract: The agency did not complete a practice countdown for a flight that would be the first to send people around the moon in more than 50 years.

multimedia.caption: The Artemis II rocket at the launch complex at Cape Canaveral, Fla. on Jan. 17.

articles.title: Snow Drought in the West Reaches Record Levels
articles.abstract: Warm temperatures and extremely low snowfall threaten water resources for the year.

multimedia.caption: Artificial snow at Soldier Hollow Nordic Center in Midway, Utah, in January.
Artificial snow at Soldier Hollow Nordic Center in Midway, Utah, in January.

articles.title: Jeff Bezos’ Rocket Company Pauses Space Tourism to Focus on the Moon
articles.abstract: The New Shepard rocket from Blue Origin, which brought 92 people on trips to the edge of space, will cease flying for at least two years as the company prioritizes NASA contracts.

multimedia.caption: The reusable New Shepard booster rocket falling back toward West Texas in February 2025.
The reusable New Shepard booster rocket falling back toward West Texas in February 2025.
The reusable New Shepard booster rocket falling back toward West Texas in February 2025.
The reusable New Shepard booster rocket falling back toward West Texas in February 2025.

articles.title: Researchers Are Using A.I. to Decode the Human Genome
articles.abstract: AlphaGenome is a leap forward in the ability to study the human blueprint. But the fine workings of our DNA are still largely a mystery.

multimedia.caption: DNA electrophoresis in a lab. Scientists at Google DeepMind have trained their A.I on a vast wealth of molecular data, enabling it to make predictions about thousands of genes.
DNA electrophoresis in a lab. Scientists at Google DeepMind have trained their A.I on a vast wealth of molecular data, enabling it to make predictions about thousands of genes.

articles.title: Gladys West, Unsung Figure in Development of GPS, Dies at 95
articles.abstract: As a Navy mathematician in the 1950s and beyond, she played an unheralded but foundational role in making possible the global satellite-based mapping system.

articles.title: Peter H. Duesberg, 89, Renowned Biologist Turned H.I.V. Denialist, Dies
articles.abstract: His pioneering work on the origins of cancer was later overshadowed by his contrarian views, notably his rejection of the established theory that H.I.V. causes AIDS.

multimedia.caption: Peter H. Duesberg in 1985, holding a tray of petri dishes containing cultured cancer cells. In the late 1960s, he discovered the first known cancer-causing gene, or oncogene.
multimedia.copyright: Roger Ressmeyer/Corbis — VCG, via Getty Images

articles.title: You Wouldn’t Want to Butt Heads With This Small Dinosaur
articles.abstract: A newly discovered raptor had a knobby bump on its head, suggesting that, like some larger dinosaurs, it engaged in competitive head bashing.

multimedia.caption: An artist’s interpretation of Xenovenator espinosai, a newly discovered species of cassowary-size, head-butting raptor from Mexico that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.
An artist’s interpretation of Xenovenator espinosai, a newly discovered species of cassowary-size, head-butting raptor from Mexico that lived during the Late Cretaceous period.

articles.title: Thomas Fogarty, 91, Who Helped Revolutionize Vascular Surgery, Dies
articles.abstract: Drawing on his love of fly-fishing, he developed a balloon catheter that removes blood clots from patients’ limbs in a minimally invasive way. It has saved millions of lives.

multimedia.caption: Dr. Thomas J. Fogarty in 2000 on receiving the Lemelson-M.I.T. Prize for his work in developing medical technologies. He “single-handedly changed the face of cardiovascular surgery,” a colleague said.

Dr. Thomas J. Fogarty in 2000 on receiving the Lemelson-M.I.T. Prize for his work in developing medical technologies. He “single-handedly changed the face of cardiovascular surgery,” a colleague said.

articles.title: 430,000-Year-Old Wooden Tools Are the Oldest Ever Found
articles.abstract: The finding, along with the discovery of a 500,000-year-old hammer made of bone, indicates that our human ancestors were making tools even earlier than archaeologists thought.

multimedia.caption: Small 430,000-year-old wooden tools of uncertain function recovered from a site in southern Greece.
Small 430,000-year-old wooden tools of uncertain function recovered from a site in southern Greece.
